source.title: Parietal cortex: from sight to action

confidence_rationale: Rizzolatti et al. (1997) characterise peripersonal space (PPS) as a dynamically updated near-body region with distinct neural processing in premotor area F4 and parietal area 7b/VIP. PPS neurons respond to stimuli within reaching distance, encode objects in body-part-centered (egocentric) coordinates, and gate motor responses to near-body events. This directly grounds MECH-097: the commit boundary coincides with the PPS boundary, where z_self (body-centered) and z_world (allocentric) first interact. In CausalGridWorld, contamination occurs exactly at this boundary — the agent's foot contacts the cell. PPS is dynamically updated (scales with tool use, motor reach, attentional state), consistent with z_beta modulating commitment thresholds (ARC-016).

mapping.source_claim_statement: Parietal areas (7b, VIP) and premotor area F4 contain neurons that respond to visual, tactile, and somatosensory stimuli within a dynamically defined near-body region — peripersonal space (PPS). PPS neurons encode object positions in body-part-centered coordinates (head-centered, arm-centered) rather than external allocentric coordinates. The PPS boundary is not fixed: it scales with tool use (extends to tool tip), motor reach, and attentional focus. Objects entering PPS trigger different neural processing from objects outside it. This near-body region is where action-execution and sensory-consequence interact: the locus at which motor commands produce world-directed effects.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-097: the commit boundary is architecturally grounded in PPS — the dynamically maintained spatial region where z_self (body-state, motor-sensory, E2's domain) and z_world (external environment, E3's domain) first interact. Actions that cross the PPS boundary (agent foot contacting cell, hand contacting object) constitute the class of events that generate z_self→z_world causal transitions and thereby moral residue. In CausalGridWorld, the contamination event is precisely a PPS-boundary crossing: the agent's positional state (z_self) intersects the cell's state (z_world). PPS is dynamically modulated (scales with attentional state, motor context) — in REE terms, this means the commit locus is a z_beta-modulated threshold, not a fixed spatial constant. This connects MECH-097 to ARC-016 (dynamic precision gating of commitment): z_beta affects both E3 heartbeat rate (MECH-093) and the effective reach of the PPS boundary (MECH-097), providing a unified affective modulation of commitment sensitivity.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Rizzolatti et al. characterise PPS in terms of neural encoding in macaque premotor and parietal cortex. The mapping to a latent-space commit boundary is a REE architectural inference. The specific claim that PPS boundary crossing generates moral residue is not in the source — the source establishes PPS as the action-execution/sensory-consequence interface; the moral-residue application is REE-specific. CausalGridWorld's contamination events are a simplified proxy for PPS boundary crossing.
mapping.source_context: Review of macaque single-unit recording studies in premotor area F4 and parietal area 7b/VIP; introduces the peripersonal space concept as distinct from extrapersonal space; connects to mirror neuron system (F5) for action observation. Basis for subsequent human neuroimaging literature on PPS and body ownership.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality — foundational PPS paper with robust single-unit evidence. Mapping fidelity is moderate: PPS as commit locus is structurally plausible but is an inferential step beyond the source. Transfer risk is higher than MECH-095/096 because the moral-residue application requires two mappings (PPS → commit boundary, commit boundary → residue trigger) that are both REE-specific.
